content.title: Generalized Eigenvector
content.content:
Let $T: V \to V$ be a linear operator and $\lambda$ an eigenvalue. 
A nonzero vector $v$ is a **generalized eigenvector** 
of $T$ corresponding to $\lambda$ if
\[
(T - \lambda I)^k v = 0
\]
for some positive integer $k$. The smallest such $k$ is the 
**rank** of the generalized eigenvector.

content.title: Generalized Eigenspace
content.content:
The **generalized eigenspace** of $\lambda$ is
\[
G_\lambda = \{v \in V : (T - \lambda I)^k v = 0 \text{ for some } k \geq 1\}
\]
Equivalently, $G_\lambda = \ker(T - \lambda I)^n$ where $n = \dim V$.

content.title: Generalized Eigenspace Dimension
content.content: The dimension of $G_\lambda$ equals the algebraic multiplicity of $\lambda$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Generalized Eigenspace Decomposition
content.content:
Let $T: V \to V$ be a linear operator on a complex vector space with 
eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_k$. Then
\[
V = G_{\lambda_1} \oplus G_{\lambda_2} \oplus \cdots \oplus G_{\lambda_k}
\]
Each $G_{\lambda_i}$ is $T$-invariant.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Nilpotent Operator
content.content:
An operator $N: V \to V$ is **nilpotent** if 
$N^k = 0$ for some positive integer $k$. The smallest such $k$ is 
the **index of nilpotency**.

content.title: Nilpotent Structure
content.content:
Let $N$ be nilpotent on an $n$-dimensional space. Then:

1. $N^n = 0$

2. The only eigenvalue of $N$ is $0$

3. The characteristic polynomial is $p_N(\lambda) = (-\lambda)^n$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Jordan Form for Nilpotent Operators
content.content:
Every nilpotent operator has a matrix representation that is 
block-diagonal, where each block is of the form
\[
J_m(0) = \begin{pmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0
\end{pmatrix}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Jordan Block
content.content:
A **Jordan block** of size $m$ with eigenvalue 
$\lambda$ is the $m \times m$ matrix
\[
J_m(\lambda) = \begin{pmatrix}
\lambda & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & \lambda & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & \cdots & \lambda & 1 \\
0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & \lambda
\end{pmatrix} = \lambda I + J_m(0)
\]

content.title: Jordan Chain
content.content:
A **Jordan chain** of length $m$ for 
eigenvalue $\lambda$ is a sequence of vectors $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_m$ such that
$$
(T - \lambda I)v_1 &= 0 \\
(T - \lambda I)v_2 &= v_1 \\
&\vdots \\
(T - \lambda I)v_m &= v_{m-1}
$$

content.title: Jordan Chain Basis
content.content:
If $(v_1, \ldots, v_m)$ is a Jordan chain, then these vectors are 
linearly independent, and the matrix of $T$ restricted to their 
span, with respect to the basis $(v_m, v_{m-1}, \ldots, v_1)$, is 
$J_m(\lambda)$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Jordan Canonical Form
content.content:
Let $T: V \to V$ be a linear operator on a finite-dimensional complex 
vector space. There exists a basis of $V$ with respect to which the 
matrix of $T$ is block-diagonal:
\[
J = \begin{pmatrix}
J_{m_1}(\lambda_1) & & & \\
& J_{m_2}(\lambda_2) & & \\
& & \ddots & \\
& & & J_{m_r}(\lambda_r)
\end{pmatrix}
\]
This **Jordan canonical form** is unique up to permutation of blocks.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Uniqueness of Jordan Form
content.content:
Two matrices are similar if and only if they have the same Jordan 
canonical form (up to ordering of blocks).
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Determining Jordan Structure
content.content:
For eigenvalue $\lambda$:

- Number of Jordan blocks = $\dim \ker(T - \lambda I)$ = geometric multiplicity

- Number of blocks of size $\geq k$ = $\dim \ker(T - \lambda I)^k - \dim \ker(T - \lambda I)^{k-1}$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Computing Jordan Form
content.problem:
Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$.

Eigenvalues: $\lambda = 2$ (algebraic mult. 2), $\lambda = 3$ (mult. 1).

For $\lambda = 2$: $\ker(A - 2I) = \ker\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$
has dimension 1 (geometric mult.).

Since algebraic = 2 but geometric = 1, there is one Jordan block of size 2.

Jordan form: $J = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ 
(the matrix is already in Jordan form!).
